To address the issue of wrong order of token embeddings, we perform several discrete sequence transformations.
The performance of discrete sequence transformations is evaluated based on both a low reconstruction loss and a low perplexity under the auxiliary language model.
The performance of discrete sequence transformations is evaluated based on both a low reconstruction loss and a low perplexity under the auxiliary language model.
Candidates for the new reconstruction are generated from the current reconstruction.
Swap transformation selects two positions i and j uniformly at random in the sequence to swap tokens xi and xj.
MoveToken transformation selects two positions i and j uniformly at random in the sequence to move the token xi after position j.
MoveSubseq transformation selects three positions i, j, and p uniformly at random in the sequence to move the subsequence of tokens between i and j after position p.
MovePreﬁx transformation selects a position i uniformly at random in the sequence to move the preﬁx of the sequence ending at position i to the end of the sequence.
A language model is used to check if generated candidates improve over the current sequence.

Reconstruction loss is used in the Complete Reconstruction Attack algorithm to iteratively update the input x.
In the Complete Reconstruction Attack, the Lbest is calculated as the sum of reconstruction loss and αlm multiplied by perplexity.
The parameter αlm determines the trade-off between the reconstruction loss and perplexity. It can influence the behavior of the attack towards reconstructing or focusing on perplexity.
In the Complete Reconstruction Attack algorithm, x is iteratively updated by subtracting the gradient of the reconstruction loss.
